title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoors Hot Line (Published: March 16, 2003) Homer will hold its 10th annual winter king salmon tournament on Saturday. Lines can go in the water at 8 a.m. during this one-day fishing extravaganza and must be out of the water by 4 p.m. Organizers plan to give away more than $50,000 in cash and merchandise. For a festival packet, which includes rules, an entry form and everything else you need to know, contact the Homer Chamber of Commerce at 1-907-235-7740. They will mail or fax you the information. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ketchikan dogs fall prey to wolf pack, biologist says HUNGRY: Lack of snow makes it harder for predators to kill deer. The Associated Press (Published: March 6, 2003) Ketchikan -- Hungry wolves are killing dogs in the Ketchikan area, according to a state wildlife management biologist. At least four dogs have been killed in the past month, Boyd Porter said. It appears that a pack that has always come close to town now has become skilled at killing dogs, he said. Some dogs were accompanied by people, Porter said. The four deaths occurred north of the city. Though it's not unusual to have wolves kill and eat one or two dogs annually, the numbers appear higher this year, he said, probably because the wolves are having a harder time finding deer to eat. A lack of snow this winter has allowe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Moose tramples runner MICHAEL VOGEL: Masters skier says he is lucky to be alive. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 9, 2003) In the blink of an eye, masters skier Michael Vogel last week went from jogging along the Anchorage Coastal Trail to down in the brush beside it -- bloody, in shock and pain, wondering if he would survive. Seconds earlier, Vogel had come upon a moose where the trail bridges a creek at the west end of the east-west runway at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport. The moose, Vogel remembers, was browsing yards off the trail. It was apparently a cow, Vogel said. Because bull moose drop their horns in the winter, it can be difficult to tell them from cows this time of year, but Vogel said this animal appeared to be with a calf. A smaller moo…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Disrupting flight costs Houston man $38,000 SENTENCE: Incident diverted Northwest plane to Anchorage. By Nicole Tsong Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 11, 2003) Matthew Leggett's claim that he was a terrorist on a Northwest Airlines flight diverted to Anchorage last year just cost him at least $38,000. That's how much U.S. Magistrate John Roberts ordered him Monday to pay Northwest, a sum the airline says in court documents was less than half of what the diversion of Flight 19 from Minneapolis to Tokyo cost. Roberts also sentenced the Houston man to five years of probation, the prosecution said. The judge prohibited Leggett, 42, from flying on commercial airlines for the first year of his probation. For the remaining four years, he must get permission from his probation officer t…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Coast Guard searches for freighter that struck boat The Associated Press (Published: March 4, 2003) ANCHORAGE (AP) - A 101-foot crab boat and a freighter, estimated to be about 500 feet long, collided near Akun Island in the Bering Sea early Monday, the U.S. Coast Guard said. No injuries were reported, but the collision crushed a 15-foot section of the crabber's bow. The damage to the freighter was unknown as the ship never stopped. The Kodiak-based crabber, The Katrina Em, was able to sail to Dutch Harbor safely despite the damage. The Coast Guard used aircraft to search for the freighter, which was believed to be traveling west. Get more Alaska news at adn.com Sponsors

summary: In Alaska Outdoors News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Business | Sports Field Notes (Published: March 2, 2003) FISHING Rainbow stocking cut at Lower Fire Lake Alaska's Department of Fish and Game has announced it will cut in half the stocking of rainbow trout in Lower Fire Lake near Eagle River because pike appear to be taking over the lake. In 2001, the Division of Sport Fish stocked more than 3,000 catchable-size rainbows in the lake. Anglers reported catching only 366 rainbow trout, compared to more than 3,900 northern pike. "Our hatchery rainbow trout are in such high demand by sport anglers that we can't afford to stock them as pike food," said Matt Miller, an area management biologist with the state. For more information, contact Miller at 267-2415. Survey results on K…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Coastal Trail comments due May 16 By Rosemary Shinohara Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 7, 2003) The state is drawing out the comment period on routes for the proposed extension of Anchorage's Coastal Trail once again. It was supposed to have ended today. The new deadline is May 16. The state Department of Transportation commissioner added more than two months because the project is so controversial and the state wants everyone with an opinion to have a chance to send it in, said John Manly, spokesman for Gov. Frank Murkowski. On the table is a draft environmental impact report that supports a mostly coastal route to extend the Tony Knowles Coastal Trail 13 miles from Kincaid Park to Potter Marsh. The trail is expe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Evergreen eyes Valley coal beds METHANE: Pioneer unit hopes to launch production by year's end. Petroleum News Alaska (Published: March 12, 2003) Lower 48 coal-bed methane producer Evergreen Resources, liking what it saw in last year's Alaska drilling results, says it's planning a third, four-well pilot project this year to test deeper coal at its 48,000-acre Pioneer unit near Houston in Mat-Su. Mark Sexton, Evergreen's chief executive, also told analysts in a Feb. 27 conference that Evergreen is hoping to prove up sufficient gas reserves by year's end to launch production. The Pioneer unit would be Alaska's first commercial coal-bed methane operation. Evergreen plans to spend $7 million to $8 million in Alaska this year, compared to $6 million in 2002, the company told analysts. The b…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Dogs help out in class for disabled students THERAPY: Animals provide "sensory stimulation for kids." By Jenni Dillon Peninsula Clarion (Published: November 6, 2002) Kenai -- The kids in Teresa Owens' class don't do a lot of running and jumping. But that doesn't mean they don't have their own brand of fun. Owens teaches special education at Kalifornsky Beach Elementary. The five students assigned to her intensive needs class have a range of disabilities both physical and mental. Some come to class in wheelchairs and move only with the help of teachers and aides. Some stare toward the ceiling, displaying varying degrees of autism and often ignoring other people. But they perk up around Nellie and Maggie, Owens' golden retrievers -- and an increasingly visible part of the K-Beach communi…

This display is at St. Innocent Cathedral on Turpin Street at East Fourth Avenue. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ban remains on use of Native names for school mascots UPHELD: Such nicknames are derogatory, says Seattle judge ruling against alumni. The Associated Press (Published: March 12, 2003) Seattle -- A ban on the use of American Indian and Alaska Native names for school mascots and nicknames has been upheld by a King County Superior Court judge. Judge James A. Doerty upheld the city school board's policy Monday against a challenge by the West Seattle High School Alumni Association, which claimed the ban was discriminatory and violated the equal-protection clause of the 14th Amendment. The constitutional guarantee of fundamental rights to life, liberty and property does not apply to the case because the school policy deals with mascots rather than with any specific individual, Doerty ruled. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Stevens fights plan to cut rural air subsidy SERVICE: Senator doesn't want to bill Alaska communities. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 12, 2003) Washington -- Sen. Ted Stevens objected Tuesday to an administration plan that would reduce a federal subsidy airlines receive for flying to small cities and villages. Air travel to 33 Alaska communities -- and 92 elsewhere in the country -- is subsidized through the Essential Air Service Program. The U.S. Department of Transportation says communities should kick in at least 10 percent of the subsidy. Stevens, a Republican, fumed at a Senate hearing Tuesday that the proposal is unfair to Alaska because most of the communities served by the program there have no access other than by plane. "Why do you want any contribution …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | State cuts would cost city $4 million FUNDING: Social services hit hard; mayor supports plan, vows to find money. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 12, 2003) Anchorage city government and social service agencies would lose about $4 million in state funding next year if Gov. Frank Murkowski's budget is approved, city officials said Tuesday. The cuts are part of Murkowski's proposal to save about $9 million by trimming a revenue sharing program with cities and eliminating a small program that aids such nonprofit programs as food banks, low-income health care and sexual assault centers in Anchorage and Fairbanks. The 25 percent cut to revenue sharing will eliminate $2.5 million in general funds to the city government, about 1 percent of the city budget. Twenty-two local…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Judge gives hatchet killer 99 years LAMAR GATHERS: Murderer killed Eagle River teen and seriously wounded another. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 22, 2003) The victim's family wanted him killed. The prosecutor suggested 105 years or more in prison. Then the defendant made it nearly unanimous, telling the judge to give him the maximum. "Serving life without parole should be the sentence," Lamar Gathers said Friday before a packed courtroom at the end of a four-hour sentencing hearing. On Aug. 28, 2001, Gathers, then 19, climbed a ladder into an Eagle River home and hacked Dustin Gard to death with a hatchet. He sliced away at Gard's girlfriend, Bridgette Blezinski, opening her head and severing the bone in her forearm, but she talked him out of finishing her …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Heavy seas turn kayak outing upside down SAFE: Rescuers find 3 Anchorage men stranded on beach near Whittier. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 18, 2003) Three Anchorage kayakers were rescued from a cold beach Saturday night after a winter outing from Whittier into Prince William Sound turned into an ordeal, Alaska State Troopers said Monday. Daniel L. Heath, 27; Michael F. Jones, 42; and Shane Moore, 25, were found in good health despite exhausting themselves against a powerful headwind in heavy seas. "It was damn near impossible paddling," Heath said Monday night. The three were lucky, said Larry Gilman, owner of a Whittier marine service. Gilman and other people, including a Whittier police officer, went out on Gilman's boat and found the three men on Billings…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Economist testifies in price-fixing fisheries suit The Associated Press (Published: February 28, 2003) ANCHORAGE (AP) - An economist testified Friday evidence exists there was a conspiracy to fix prices in the Bristol Bay sockeye salmon markets. Jeffrey Leitzinger, a Los Angeles economist and expert witness for plaintiffs in a $1 billion class action lawsuit alleging price fixing, said from the mid-1980s through 1995 there was about a 95 cent per pound overall price decline, most of it borne by fishermen. "Putting the conspiracy explanation aside, it is hard to explain," he said. The trial in Superior Court in Anchorage stems from a lawsuit filed in 1995 on behalf of 4,500 Bristol Bay permit holders. It contends that Seat…

summary: In Alaska Business News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Sports Marketing Quality By DAN JOLING The Associated Press (Published: March 3, 2003) In the new world of Alaska regional seafood marketing, sockeye salmon will be on ice seconds after they're plucked from the sea. They'll be coddled like newborns to prevent bruising. And they'll have brand names that remind customers of where they come from: Aleutia. Kenai Wild. Copper River. Kodiak. Desperate to revive sales that have tanked because of sluggish foreign economies and competition from farmed fish, some Alaska salmon fishermen are banding together to create regional brands for customers willing to pay a premium for fresh, wild fish. summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Aides named in Capitol offices By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 3, 2003) Washington -- Sen. Lisa Murkowski has named three more key aides as she builds her staff here. Jon DeVore began working last week as her chief counsel and legislative director. He will oversee all legislation the senator works on but will focus on Native, rural and economic development issues, chief of staff Justin Stiefel said. DeVore had been working as a U.S. attorney for the Small Business Administration in Anchorage. In 1981, he worked as an aide for then freshman Sen. Frank Murkowski and before that for Sen. summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports More gold boosts life of Fort Knox mine The Associated Press (Published: February 27, 2003) FAIRBANKS(AP) - Fairbanks Gold Mining found more gold than expected in 2002 and the additional gold will extend the life of the Fort Knox mine by two years, general manager Rick Dye said Wednesday. Operations at the mine would end in 2010 rather than 2008, as previously expected. The company is working to boost its number of employees from 388 to 425 this year, as the price of gold remains relatively high. The company said it is spending $11 million on the expansion of its operating fleet. "2002 was the best year that Fort Knox has had for adding resources," Dye said. summary: In Alaska Business News More headlines » Alaska | National | World | Sports City's inflation rate climbs By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 22, 2003) Anchorage's inflation rate slightly outpaced the national average last year, as rising housing costs offset declines in transportation and clothing prices, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Friday. Consumer prices around Anchorage rose 1.9 percent on average in the past year, according to the bureau's consumer price index, or CPI, a closely watched measure of changes in housing, food, transportation, clothing and other common costs. The increase in Anchorage last year was on par with other cities in the West, but above an average 1.6 percent increase for the nation. Clothing prices fell sharply, and over…

summary: In Alaska News More headlines » National | World | Business | Sports Experts duel over print By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 27, 2003) A print in Della Brown's blood found near her body on a piece of paper may not actually be a fingerprint and definitely was not made by defendant Joshua Wade. Or maybe it was. It also was not made by Brown's hand but could have been from her toe. Or maybe it was from her hand after all. Who knows. It's hard to imagine what jurors in Wade's trial for the murder of Brown made of testimony from dueling fingerprint experts offered in court Tuesday and Wednesday. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Forest plans please loggers DECISIONS: Agency recommends Tongass wilderness be capped. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published: March 1, 2003) Despite an outpouring of public support for more wilderness in the Tongass National Forest, the federal agency that manages the giant Southeast Alaska rain forest said Friday that no more land should be set aside for conservation. But Forest Service officials also said Congress should carve out 1.4 million acres of the Chugach National Forest and make it wilderness, a designation that bars most development such as logging and road building. The area, called Nellie Juan/College Fiord, straddles the western side of Prince William Sound. If Congress approves the designation, it will be the first wilderness created in the nation since 1984, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Pipeline cleanup near end SPILL: Costs, including labor and recovery of crude, total $20 million. The Associated Press Wire (Published: April 7, 2002) Fairbanks -- The cost of repairing the trans-Alaska oil pipeline and cleaning up a 286,000-gallon oil spill caused by a bullet hole has climbed to $20 million, according to Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. The company has nearly finished cleaning up the oil spilled north of Fairbanks last October after a Livengood man allegedly shot a hole in the pipeline with a high-caliber rifle. All that remains is less than 1,000 gallons of oil trapped in the gravel pad of the pipeline's access road, said Kalu Kalu, Alyeska project manager. "We're pumping it into barrels," he said. About $6 million of the $20 million cleanup bill was for labor costs and …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Fisherman says processor 'put screws to us' BRISTOL BAY: Nelbro didn't deliver services it promised, witness says. The Associated Press (Published: February 26, 2003) A Bristol Bay fisherman testified Tuesday that a major processor quashed his 1995 effort to bring a new, independent processing vessel on line in the Bristol Bay sockeye sal-mon fishery. Frederick S. Magill of Sitka, testifying in the Bristol Bay price-fixing case, said Nelbro Packing Co. loaned his company, Baypack Fisheries, more than $1.5 million for conversion of a processing vessel, but failed to deliver promised technical support or good prices from Japanese buyers. Baypack went out of business after one season. Baypack eventually sued Nelbro and settled out of court. The class-action price fixing trial began Feb. 3…

(Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Assemblyman Dan Kendall, offended by a big, bold, red auto supply sign in Eagle River, decided a year ago that there ought to be a law about it. So he wrote an ordinance calling for a citywide ban on signs stuck way up on poles. "Mine is pretty simple," Kendall said. No pole signs and no signs at all more than 10 feet high. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Red Dog appeal will get its day SUPREME COURT: Justices to look at power struggle between state, EPA. The Associated Press (Published: February 25, 2003) The U.S. Supreme Court on Monday agreed to hear an appeal from the state of Alaska that forces the Red Dog mine to use expensive anti-pollution technology for its power generation. At issue is whether the Environmental Protection Agency has the power to overrule a state's day-to-day decisions on national environmental rules. Gov. Frank Murkowski said Monday that he was pleased with the high court's decision to hear the case. "I think it will hopefully resolve the authority of the state with regard to air quality," Murkowski said. "We're going to get the thing resolved once and for all." State officials filed a federal lawsuit two year…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Acquaintance testifies that Wade showed him Brown's body ABSENT: Assistant DA takes over questioning for ailing prosecutor. By Sheila Toomey Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 21, 2003) Assistant District Attorney Mary Anne Henry takes part in the Joshua Wade trial earlier this month. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Daily News Archive 2003) Click on photo to enlarge After seven days on the witness stand, Timothy Beckett yielded the hot seat Thursday to another young man who says defendant Joshua Wade showed him Della Brown's dead body before police found it in an abandoned Spenard shed. Jonathan McCune, now 19, said he had no idea what sight awaited him when Wade led him to the shed and flicked a lighter to reveal the bloody body of a woman. "Her face was facing directly toward me," Mc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Good Neighbors (Published: February 24, 2003) The Alaska Professional Disc Golf Association, along with generous support from A-1 Fence, Play-It-Again Sports, Coca-Cola of Alaska, Acme Fence and Oxford Gold & Precious Metals, collected $750 for Bean's Cafe as a result of its first Alaska State Disc Golf Championships held in September. First National Bank and its 750 employees statewide donated more than $1.2 million in 2002 in charitable gifts, activity sponsorships and in-kind donations of time and talent. Financial support was contributed to various health, youth, educational, civic and arts organizations statewide. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | GOP supports local partisan races CHANGE: Many charters don't allow candidates to list party on city ballots. By Ben Spiess Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 24, 2003) The Republican Party wants to increase its involvement in nonpartisan local elections, party chairman Randy Ruedrich says. He would like to have Republican candidates earmarked for all elected municipal positions, including for school board, assembly and mayor. "It would be extremely helpful to give people an identity of who they are voting for," Ruedrich said. However, most municipal and borough elections in Alaska, including those in Anchorage, are designated specifically as nonpartisan under local government charters and codes. Anchorage's simply says, "All municipal elections shall be nonpartisan." Ruedrich d…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | ANWR crude may be light, sweet OIL: U.S. Geological Survey says samples taken from ANWR are low-sulfur. The Associated Press (Published: February 18, 2003) While debate continues over whether to open the coastal plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to petroleum drilling, government geologists say the oil underneath may be the sweet, low-sulfur crude in high demand by refiners. "The oil we've studied in ANWR is higher gravity and lower sulfur oil than oil in Prudhoe Bay," U.S. Geological Survey research geologist Ken Bird told Petroleum News Alaska. "Prudhoe Bay-type oil contains 1 to 2 percent sulfur, while samples from ANWR measure between zero and 1 percent sulfur." That would make some of coastal plain oil 10 times lighter than Prudhoe Bay crude. Oil tested by USGS was gathe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Senate plan curbs funding for trails STATE: Federal dollars would be shifted to roads, not enhancements. By Sean Cockerham Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 28, 2003) Juneau -- State Sen. Ben Stevens wants to cut back by millions the share of federal highway dollars that go for a program then-Gov. Tony Knowles created to build trails, waysides, rest stops and other transportation amenities. His proposed legislation, Senate Bill 71, also would limit how much federal money Anchorage and Fairbanks can spend on trails and related projects. "It is to make more money available to local municipalities for construction of roads instead of construction of trails," said Stevens, a South Anchorage Republican. The bill would shift an estimated $13 million each year from trails and other "e…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ben Stevens won't testify in Bristol Bay case SALMON: Judge denies motion to place state senator on stand. By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: February 28, 2003) State Sen. Ben Stevens will not testify in the Bristol Bay salmon civil trial under way in Superior Court in Anchorage. Judge Peter Michalski this week denied a motion from one of the defendants in the billion-dollar case, Trident Seafoods Corp. of Seattle, to put Stevens on the witness stand. The judge didn't explain his decision. Lawyers for fishermen accusing a group of processors and Japanese fish importers of price fixing complained that Trident wanted to call Stevens as a "grandstanding" move. They said Trident's lawyers missed a court-imposed deadline for adding names to their witness list. Stevens, R-Anchora…
